messages.content: لطفا متن زیر را به زبان انگلیسی به گونه ایی بازنویسی کن که معنی و مفهوم آن حفظ شود و تغییر نکند. In recent years, the use of electrochemical techniques for quantifying important analytes has grown exponentially due to the wide versatility, miniaturization, good sensitivity, great adaptability for in situ determination, and the use of inexpensive instruments and requirements in comparison with other analytical techniques.

The utilization of electrochemical approaches for measuring significant analytes has seen an exponential growth in recent years. This is primarily attributed to their versatile nature, miniaturization capabilities, high sensitivity, adaptability for in situ determination, and cost-effectiveness compared to alternative analytical methods.

Screen-printed electrodes (SPE) are very effective devices for the development of disposable sensors used in electroanalysis. SPE are mostly based on carbon materials, in which the nature, structures, and physicochemical properties of the carbonaceous materials play significant roles in sensor performance. SPCE offers several advantages, such as versatility of design, reproducibility of sensor preparation, and low production cost.50 These features allow disposal of sensors after single usage, solving the inconveniences of reusable sensors.

Screen-printed electrodes (SPE) have proven to be highly efficient tools in the creation of disposable sensors utilized in electroanalysis. These electrodes are predominantly constructed using carbon materials, where the characteristics, structures, and chemical properties of the carbonaceous materials significantly influence the performance of the sensors. The utilization of SPCE grants numerous benefits, including the ability to design sensors in a versatile manner, ensuring reproducibility during sensor preparation, and minimizing production costs. As a result, these features enable the disposal of sensors after a single use, thus addressing the drawbacks associated with reusable sensors.
On the other hand, the electrode material plays an important role in successful electrochemical detection of various analytes.
Furthermore, the choice of electrode material is crucial for the effective electrochemical detection of diverse analytes.
However, low catalytic activity, slow charge transfer kinetics, and fouling of the surface of SPE make the electrochemical performance poor in sensitivity, selectivity, and repeatability

Nevertheless, the electrochemical performance of SPE can be hindered by factors such as low catalytic activity, sluggish charge transfer kinetics, and surface fouling, leading to poor sensitivity, selectivity, and repeatability.
For this purpose, it is important to select chemically inert and electrochemically stable materials. Therefore, electrode materials with excellent performance are crucial in electrochemical methods.

To address these challenges, it becomes essential to choose electrode materials that are chemically inert and electrochemically stable. Hence, the selection of electrode materials with exceptional performance becomes imperative in electrochemical methods.
The modified electrode can increase the speed of electron transfer and current sensitivity, and minimize over-potential.
Utilizing modified electrodes can enhance the rate of electron transfer, improve current sensitivity, and minimize over-potential.

Because of their versatile electrical, mechanical, and physiochemical properties, nanomaterials, including graphene, conducting polymers,  quantum dots, and metal nanoparticles, have endowed electrochemical sensors with more possibilities in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and real-time detection.

Nanomaterials such as graphene, conducting polymers, quantum dots, and metal nanoparticles have significantly expanded the capabilities of electrochemical sensors due to their versatile electrical, mechanical, and physicochemical properties. These nanomaterials offer enhanced sensitivity, selectivity, and real-time detection, providing electrochemical sensors with greater potential.
In fact, the modification of an electrode with nanomaterials can not only increase the effective surface area, enhance the conductivity, and provide catalytic sites.

Indeed, the modification of electrodes with nanomaterials offers several advantages. It can effectively increase the surface area, improve conductivity, and create additional catalytic sites on the electrode.
Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) material is a new type of porous nanomaterial that consists of an organic linker and metal ions, and has become a popular research topic.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a novel type of porous nanomaterial that has gained significant attention in research. Comprised of organic linkers and metal ions, MOFs have emerged as a fascinating area of study.
They display very large surface areas, ordered crystalline structure,  high porosity, high thermal stability, and offer almost infinite possibilities in terms of design and architecture.

MOFs exhibit remarkably large surface areas, well-organized crystalline structures, high porosity, and excellent thermal stability. These unique characteristics open up nearly limitless possibilities for their design and architectural configurations.
These characteristics render MOFs as attractive materials for gas/energy storage, adsorption, separation, sensing, drug delivery, and catalysis.
Due to their exceptional properties, MOFs are highly sought-after for a wide range of applications including gas and energy storage, adsorption, separation, sensing, drug delivery, and catalysis.

Among different MOFs applications, MOF-based electrochemical sensors are a hopeful application. In recent years, some researchers have discussed the application of MOFs and their derivatives in sensors.

Among the various applications of MOFs, the utilization of MOF-based electrochemical sensors holds great promise. In recent years, researchers have explored the potential of MOFs and their derivatives in sensor applications.
In this work, we developed a robust electrochemical sensing method for the simultaneous determination of tramadol and morphine.
In our study, we have successfully devised a reliable electrochemical sensing technique capable of concurrently detecting tramadol and morphine.
First, a zirconium-based MOF (UiO-66-NH2) was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized MOF was characterized via different techniques.

To begin, we synthesized a zirconium-based MOF, specifically UiO-66-NH2, using a straightforward hydrothermal method. The resultant MOF was then subjected to characterization using various analytical techniques.
Then, UiO-66-NH2 was utilized as a modifier on the surface of the screen-printed graphite electrode (UiO-66-NH2/SPGE).
Subsequently, the synthesized UiO-66-NH2 was applied as a modifier on the surface of a screen-printed graphite electrode, resulting in the formation of UiO-66-NH2/SPGE.

The as-prepared electrode (UiO-66-NH2/SPGE) could effectively increase the electron transfer between the electrode and the solution, which leads to a more sensitive current response in the oxidation of tramadol under the selected experimental conditions.

The developed UiO-66-NH2/SPGE electrode proved to enhance the electron transfer between the electrode and the solution. This improvement resulted in a heightened current response during the oxidation of tramadol under the specific experimental conditions chosen for this study.
Additionally, the sensor was tested in terms of catalytic behavior for the tramadol determination in the co-existence of morphine.
Furthermore, the sensor was evaluated for its catalytic behavior in detecting tramadol in the presence of morphine.
In addition, the concentrations of tramadol and morphine in real samples are determined and satisfactory results are obtained.
Moreover, the developed sensor was employed to determine the concentrations of tramadol and morphine in real samples, yielding satisfactory and accurate results.

A practical technique was applied to fabricate MoWS2 nanocomposite through a one-pot hydrothermal method for use as the
electrocatalyst.
A practical approach was utilized to fabricate a MoWS2 nanocomposite via a one-pot hydrothermal method, with the intention of employing it as an electrocatalyst.
The characterization of MoWS2 nanocomposite was investigated by several techniques.
The MoWS2 nanocomposite was subjected to characterization using multiple techniques to assess its properties and structure.
The synthesized Uio-66-NH2 has been used to modify a screen-printed graphite electrode (Uio-66-NH2/SPGE) by a drop-casting technique.
The synthesized UiO-66-NH2 was employed to modify a screen-printed graphite electrode (UiO-66-NH2/SPGE) using a drop-casting technique.

The electrochemical features and catalytic efficiency of a Uio-66-NH2/SPGE toward tramadol oxidation were investigated using cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, chronoamperometry, and diffrential pulse voltammetry.

The electrochemical characteristics and catalytic efficiency of the UiO-66-NH2/SPGE electrode for tramadol oxidation were analyzed through various techniques, including cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, chronoamperometry, and differential pulse voltammetry.
The prepared electrode displayed linear range from 0.02 µM to 90 µM and limit of detection of 6.7 nM (S/N=3) for tramadol detection as shown by DPV results under optimized conditions.

Under optimized conditions, the prepared electrode exhibited a linear range of 0.02 µM to 90 µM for tramadol detection, as evidenced by the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) results. The limit of detection for tramadol was found to be 6.7 nM (S/N=3).
